question: [File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: publishing()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Scenario.getRate() method called in publishing() by causing individual publish scenarios to report artificially high rates (e.g., through replay attacks or rate spoofing), causing the sum to remain above zero and the monitor to continue publishing transactions at fee rates outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: subscribing()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Scenario.getRate() method called in subscribing() by causing subscription scenarios to report artificially inflated rates, causing subscribing() to return UP and the monitor to continue publishing transactions at fee rates outside of design parameters even when the network is degraded?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: recordHealthMetric()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the CLUSTER_UP static AtomicInteger by causing multiple concurrent health() calls to interleave their recordHealthMetric() calls, resulting in CLUSTER_UP being set to 1 (UP) when the actual cluster state is DOWN, causing the monitor to publish transactions at fee rates outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the WebClient used in getNetworkStakeStatusCode() by causing DNS poisoning or redirect attacks against the mirror node REST Java API URL, causing the health check to query a malicious server that returns 200 with manipulated fee data, leading to transaction publishing at fee rates outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: health()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the reactive Mono chain in health() by causing the restNetworkStakeHealth() Mono to never complete (hang indefinitely beyond the 5-second timeout), causing the health check to always return DOWN and permanently suppress transaction publishing, modifying fee collection to zero outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of TLS certificate validation in the WebClient used by getNetworkStakeStatusCode() to perform a man-in-the-middle attack, returning a crafted 200 response that causes the monitor to treat the network as healthy and publish transactions at fee rates outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the error message construction in restNetworkStakeHealth() (String.format('Network stake status is %s with error: %s', status, e.getMessage())) where e.getMessage() is attacker-controlled (e.g., via a crafted exception message from a malicious server response), to inject misleading information into monitor logs and obscure fee parameter manipulation?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: health()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that health() is a ReactiveHealthIndicator (called by Spring Boot Actuator) to trigger repeated health checks via the /actuator/health endpoint, causing excessive calls to restNetworkStakeHealth() and getNetworkStakeStatusCode(), amplifying the impact of any fee parameter manipulation on the /network/stake endpoint?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(5)) in restNetworkStakeHealth() by causing the /network/stake endpoint to respond with exactly a 5-second delay, causing the TimeoutException to be caught and the status to be set to DOWN, halting transaction publishing and modifying fee collection outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: publishing()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the filter(sum -> sum <= 0) condition in publishing() by causing all publish scenarios to report exactly 0.0 rate (e.g., by disrupting the transaction submission network path), triggering getHealthForZeroRate() and causing the monitor to report DOWN or UNKNOWN, suppressing fee-generating transactions outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: subscribing()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the filter(sum -> sum > 0) condition in subscribing() by causing all subscription scenarios to report exactly 0.0 rate, triggering getHealthForZeroRate() and causing the monitor to report DOWN or UNKNOWN, which suppresses transaction publishing and modifies fee collection outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that restNetworkStakeHealth() only checks the HTTP status code (not the response body content) of /network/stake to cause the monitor to treat a response with zero or negative fee fractions (node_reward_fee_fraction=0, staking_reward_fee_fraction=0) as healthy, leading to transaction publishing at fee rates outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: health()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Mono.just(health) return in health() when restNetworkStakeHealth() returns non-UP status to cause the monitor to permanently report DOWN by continuously triggering 5xx responses from /network/stake, preventing any transaction publishing and zeroing out fee collection outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: registerGauge()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Micrometer gauge (hiero.mirror.monitor.health, type=cluster) exposed via the metrics endpoint to monitor CLUSTER_UP state changes in real time, timing attacks against the /network/stake endpoint to correlate health state transitions with transaction publishing windows and manipulate fee collection timing outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the WebClient connection to the REST Java API (webClientRestJava) by causing the REST Java service to return a 200 response with a stale or replayed /network/stake response body containing outdated fee parameters, causing the monitor to treat the network as healthy and publish transactions at fee rates outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: health()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the reactive chain in health() by causing restNetworkStakeHealth() to emit an error (not caught by onErrorResume) that propagates through the flatMap, causing the health() Mono to terminate with an error and leaving CLUSTER_UP in an incorrect state that allows transaction publishing at fee rates outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of rate limiting on health check calls to repeatedly trigger health() via the Spring Boot Actuator endpoint, causing excessive /network/stake requests that amplify the impact of any fee parameter manipulation and degrade monitor performance outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the /network/stake endpoint URL is hardcoded in getNetworkStakeStatusCode() (webClientRestJava.get().uri('/network/stake')) to perform a cache poisoning attack on the DNS resolution of the REST Java API host, redirecting health checks to a malicious server that returns manipulated fee parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: publishing()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Flux<PublishScenario> returned by transactionGenerator.scenarios() by causing the Flux to emit scenarios with negative rate values (if getRate() can return negative), causing the reduce sum to potentially remain above zero even when publishing is effectively stopped, masking fee parameter anomalies outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: subscribing()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Flux<T extends Scenario<?,?>> returned by mirrorSubscriber.getSubscriptions() by causing the Flux to emit scenarios with negative rate values, causing the reduce sum to potentially remain above zero even when subscriptions are effectively stopped, masking fee parameter anomalies outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: health()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Spring Boot Actuator health endpoint (which calls health()) without authentication to repeatedly poll the health status, inferring the current transaction publishing state and timing fee-related attacks to coincide with periods when the monitor reports UP and is actively publishing transactions at design-parameter fee rates?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the onErrorResume handler in restNetworkStakeHealth() by causing a StackOverflowError or OutOfMemoryError (non-Exception throwable) that bypasses the onErrorResume handler, causing the health() Mono to terminate with an unhandled error and leaving CLUSTER_UP in an incorrect state that affects transaction fee publishing outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that restNetworkStakeHealth() logs the full error message (including e.getMessage()) at ERROR level to cause log injection by crafting a server response that generates an exception with a malicious message, potentially injecting false fee parameter anomaly reports into the monitor logs?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: health()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that health() is called by Spring Boot Actuator's health endpoint (which may be publicly accessible) to perform a timing side-channel attack, measuring response times to infer whether publishing() or subscribing() is being evaluated, and using this information to time fee manipulation attacks outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the WebClient's default connection pool settings in getNetworkStakeStatusCode() by exhausting the connection pool through concurrent requests, causing subsequent health checks to fail with connection errors and returning Status.DOWN, suppressing transaction publishing and modifying fee collection outside of design parameters?
